reasoning:
On August 1, 2025, a significantly weak jobs report (only 73k payrolls added, unemployment rate at 4.2%) and the announcement of new tariffs by President Trump led to a stock market selloff. These macroeconomic shocks are classic catalysts for risk-off environments, causing credit spreads to widen. The ICE BofA AAA US Corporate Index OAS jumped from ~0.31 in late July to 0.34 on Friday, August 1. Historical precedents (such as the April 2025 tariff shock) show that such initial gaps typically lead to weeks or months of elevated spreads before mean reversion occurs. 

Because we are comparing against the value on 2025-08-03 (which will likely carry the 0.34 print or a number very close to it), the short-term momentum favors a continuation of the widening. Therefore, there is a very high likelihood (~80%) that the spread will be higher than 0.34 by August 10. By September 2, the initial panic may subside, but spreads historically take around 2 months to fully digest such macro shocks; hence a 65% probability. By November 1 (three months out), the shock will likely have washed out, pulling the spread closer to its longer-term median, which sits right around 0.34–0.35, making it essentially a coin toss leaning slightly positive (53%). By January 2026, forecast uncertainty maximizes at 50%.
